summary:
The Israelite settlement of Canaan refers to the process by which the people who would become the Israelites came to inhabit the highland regions of the southern Levant, generally dated to around 1200–1050 BC. The Hebrew Bible presents this as a military conquest led by Joshua following the Exodus from Egypt, but archaeological evidence and historical analysis suggest a more complex and gradual process. Current scholarly models propose that the emergence of a distinct Israelite cultural identity in the highlands resulted from a combination of infiltration by pastoral nomads, internal migration by displaced urban Canaanites fleeing the disruptions of the [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]], and possibly the arrival of smaller groups with memories of Egyptian slavery.

Archaeological surveys of the central highlands of Canaan show a significant increase in small village settlements during the Iron Age I period, roughly 1200–1000 BC, coinciding with the period the biblical sources associate with the settlement. These villages share distinctive material characteristics — the four-room house, collar-rim storage jars, and the absence of pork bones — that differentiate them from contemporaneous Canaanite lowland settlements. These features later became associated with Israelite identity, though scholars debate whether they were ethnically exclusive markers at the time of their initial adoption.

The period of settlement corresponds roughly to the era described in the biblical Book of Judges, which presents a decentralized society of tribal groups governed by local leaders in a time of frequent conflict with neighboring peoples. By around 1050 BC this period gave way to the emergence of a unified monarchy under Saul and subsequently David, leading to the [[event:62|Kingdom of Israel]].
